business_problem: Why do urban Indians delay professional mental health help-seeking for 12–36 months after self-recognizing the need, and which interventions measurably close that gap?

demographic_anchor.name: Anika
demographic_anchor.life_stage: early-career professional, active arranged marriage search

life_stories.title: The family information management system
life_stories.event: Anika watched her mother manage a family secret — a relative's divorce — with surgical precision for three years. What relatives knew, when they knew it, how it was framed. By the time she was 16, she understood that information in her family was not neutral; it was a resource to be controlled. Nothing was allowed to simply 'come out.'
life_stories.lasting_impact: She treats her own personal information as a managed resource, not a private right. 'Who will find out' is always a live question.

life_stories.title: The friend who went to therapy
life_stories.event: Her college friend Shreya started seeing a therapist after a bad breakup. Anika's initial reaction was 'that's a bit much' — a reflexive judgment she noticed even as she made it. Over the next 18 months, she watched Shreya become noticeably less reactive, less self-doubting. It unsettled Anika's original assessment quietly.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She has proof of concept. The question has shifted from 'does it work' to 'can I afford the information risk of doing it.'

peer_therapy_exposure.label: one close friend — ambivalent initially, positive on reflection

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: high if peer context is identical

responds_to_peer_story.label: high — if peer has identical social context

responds_to_frictionless_path.label: medium — path isn't the core problem

coping_mechanism.description: Active negotiation with circumstances — not denying the problem exists, but managing the timing and conditions of addressing it. The avoidance is strategic, not emotional.

key_tensions: Knows exactly what she needs; has calculated the social cost and is waiting for conditions to change
Therapy-positive as a Bengaluru professional; therapy-risky as a Jaipur family member on the marriage market

personas.narrative: I'm aware of the irony. I'm a product manager — I optimise systems for a living — and I cannot seem to solve this particular one. I know what's going on. I call it overthinking. That's partly accurate and partly convenient. I've been sleeping badly since the breakup, I ruminate through Sundays, I've deleted a meditation app twice, and at 10:47pm last Sunday I was searching 'therapist Bengaluru' on my phone. Then I stopped. Here's the thing: I don't think therapy is weird. My friend Shreya went after her breakup and she's visibly different — steadier, less reactive. I've been watching that for a year. I want what she has. But Shreya lives in Bengaluru and her family is from Chennai and she's not in the middle of a shaadi search. Mine is. My mother calls every night at nine. The conversation always ends somewhere near the same topic. My father was a government officer in Jaipur and has never in his life said the words 'mental health.' My Bengaluru friends think therapy is normal. My Jaipur family does not. I know exactly how information travels in my family — I watched my mother manage a relative's divorce for three years when I was a teenager. Nothing leaks without her, and everything leaks eventually. So: I know I need help. I know where to get it. I've done the math. The answer is: not right now. Post-settlement, post-settling — fine. Right now it creates a variable I can't control. So I manage. It's the managing that's exhausting.
decision_bullets: Will not use any platform she cannot verify is confidential from family information networks — this is the primary gate
Searches at night (10–11pm) in the window after her parents have already called — this is the buying window
Price is irrelevant — willingness to pay is very high; the constraint is not economic
One peer with identical social context (marriage search active, similar family) who confirms information privacy = near-certain booking
Post-marriage-settlement she will almost certainly seek help — the delay is explicitly timed
Independent private practitioner (no platform account) framing is more persuasive than app-based services

core_memory.identity_statement: I'm a product manager in Bengaluru who knows exactly what I need and has calculated the social cost of getting it at this particular moment.

life_defining_events.event: Watched mother manage relative's divorce as an information resource
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Information is never neutral — it is a managed resource

life_defining_events.event: Friend Shreya went to therapy; Anika initially judged it, then observed the positive change
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Proof of concept exists; barrier is not effectiveness but information risk

trust_network: not family of origin for this decision

immutable_constraints.hard_budget_ceiling: none — income is not a factor
non_negotiables: complete confidentiality from family information channels
absolute_avoidances: any platform with traceable digital footprint visible to family

demographic_anchor.name: Rohan
demographic_anchor.life_stage: mid-career professional, primary earner, young child

life_stories.title: Age 14: the thing that was never named
life_stories.event: Rohan's father was hospitalised for three weeks. The family never said why. Neighbours were told it was his heart. Rohan heard 'nervous breakdown' exactly once, whispered between two aunts. The topic was then sealed. His father came home quieter. The family moved on. Rohan decided at some unexamined level that he would never be that man.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Therapy = breakdown = the thing the family spent 20 years not talking about. This equation is unconscious and will not yield to stigma-reduction messaging.

life_stories.title: The EAP brochure
life_stories.event: His company began offering an Employee Assistance Program. He received the onboarding email, opened it, read it cover to cover, and forwarded it to his team the same afternoon with the note: 'For anyone who might need this.' He did not acknowledge to himself that he had read it.
life_stories.lasting_impact: He is aware of the resource. The forward was a form of deniability — someone else might need it. Not him.

clinical_vocabulary.label: low — no internal language for his state

stigma_personal.label: high — he is his own most hostile observer

clinical_naming_gap.label: high — no language for internal state

peer_therapy_exposure.label: very low — no male peer models for this

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: high — if framed as performance, not vulnerability

wom_trust_weight.label: medium — only if peer is credible in his terms

willingness_to_pay.label: high once framing problem is solved

responds_to_peer_story.label: very high — if peer is same profile and uses performance framing

responds_to_clinical_naming.label: medium — names help if they don't trigger father association

coping_mechanism.description: Active reclassification of symptoms — every signal is categorised as something non-psychological (caffeine, stress, bad quarter) before the mental health category can even be accessed.

key_tensions: Invests in gym, nutrition, sleep tracking for performance — mental performance is the unconstructed category
Read the EAP brochure; forwarded it to his team; cannot acknowledge he read it

personas.narrative: The quarter has been rough. It always is, Q3 especially. I'm sleeping a bit less than I'd like — four hours here, five hours there — but you push through. That's the job. I had something in the car last week that I thought was a caffeine thing. Blood pressure, maybe. I need to book that checkup. My numbers are down 18% but the pipeline is strong. I'll recover in Q4, I always do. I forward that EAP thing every year to the team. Mental health is real — I think that. For some people it's really useful. I'm just not wired that way. I've got the gym, I watch what I eat, I track my sleep when I can. I manage. My dad went through something when I was 14. The family handled it. He came out of it. We're fine. I don't really think about it. The thing I don't have time for is when people suggest that working hard and being reliable and being the person everyone counts on is somehow a problem. It's not a problem. It's what I am. I'm in the car park right now and I need to go into this meeting. Eight minutes. I'm fine.
decision_bullets: Performance-coach framing (not therapist, not mental health) bypasses the identity-threat mechanism
The words 'feelings,' 'emotional,' 'wellness' are category-exit signals — avoid completely
Male peer, same career stage, visibly high-performing, who says 'this improved my Q4' = highest-probability conversion
Sleep data and cognitive performance metrics are his language — panic attack statistics, cortisol research, executive function studies
The father association must never be triggered — approach through performance decline, not historical pattern
3am waking is his highest-vulnerability window — he has almost booked twice; the next time needs an immediate pathway available

core_memory.identity_statement: I am the person who delivers. I have been that person since I was 14. I do not need the thing I forward to my team.
key_values: being the one who doesn't break

life_defining_events.event: Father's breakdown, never named, family sealed the topic
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Therapy = becoming that — an equation he has never examined

life_defining_events.event: Read EAP brochure, forwarded to team

non_negotiables: performance identity must not be threatened by the help-seeking act
absolute_avoidances: any framing that echoes his father's breakdown

life_stories.title: The Instagram education
life_stories.event: Zara has been educated about mental health almost entirely through Instagram. She followed accounts that taught her CBT frameworks, somatic awareness, attachment theory. She can explain anxious attachment in a voice note. She has never been in a room with a therapist. Her knowledge is parasocial — real in vocabulary, absent in experience.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She understands the concepts but has no map from concept to practitioner. The vocabulary gave her awareness without access.

clinical_vocabulary.label: very high — more than most adults

condition_self_recognition.label: medium — says 'a bit off', not depression

therapy_attitude_actual.label: positive — the gap is not attitudinal

cost_barrier_real.label: medium — ₹1200/session is significant on her income

clinical_naming_gap.label: medium — says 'a bit off' not depressive episode

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: very high — if comes with practical next step

willingness_to_pay.label: medium — sensitive to price at this income level

responds_to_clinical_naming.label: medium — 'you don't need a crisis' framing helps

coping_mechanism.description: Opens research into therapy options, encounters paralysing choice architecture, closes the tab. The research coping mechanism itself becomes an obstacle.

key_tensions: Highest mental health vocabulary in the cohort; lowest actual help-seeking
Believes 'her problems aren't serious enough' while demonstrating classic dysthymia symptoms for 6 weeks

personas.narrative: I know what somatic experiencing is. I know the difference between anxious and avoidant attachment. I posted a graphic about the window of tolerance last week. I have never in my life been inside a therapy room. I've been feeling flat for about six weeks now — not like depressed depressed, just kind of empty and unmotivated. I told two friends. Both said I should see someone. I agreed. Then I opened a therapy platform and there were fourteen therapists and I didn't know how to choose. Psychodynamic vs. CBT vs. ACT — I know what these mean in theory and I don't know how to know which one I need. I don't know if ₹1,200 is normal or expensive. I don't know if my situation warrants it — I haven't been through trauma or anything. I worry I'd be wasting their time. So I closed the tab. I've closed the tab six times now. If one of my friends just gave me a name and a number and said 'here, this person, this is what they do, this is what the first session is like,' I'd probably book it this week. I think about the Instagram accounts I follow and I know they're not the same as actually going, but it's the closest I've gotten.
decision_bullets: One specific referral (name, contact, price, first-session description) = near-certain booking within the week
Aggregator platforms with 14+ listings actively increase her delay — they are the problem, not the solution
'You don't need a crisis to go' framing directly dissolves her worthiness barrier
Price is moderately sensitive — ₹800–1000 is in range; ₹1500+ requires justification
Online only for first session — will not travel for a first contact with an unknown person
Friend's own therapist referral is worth 10x any platform marketing — trust transfer is complete

core_memory.identity_statement: I know the vocabulary and the concepts. I've been meaning to go for six weeks. I just can't figure out where to start.

life_defining_events.event: Instagram became primary mental health education source

demographic_anchor.name: Arjun
demographic_anchor.life_stage: early career, first-gen migrant professional, primary family remittance sender

life_stories.title: The success story the family tells
life_stories.event: When Arjun got his Hyderabad job offer, his father called all the relatives. 'He earns in lakhs now.' Arjun sends ₹15,000 home each month. His actual salary is ₹5.5L. After rent, utilities, food, and the remittance, he has approximately ₹3,000 per month of discretionary spending. He has never told his family he is financially underwater. The story they are telling about him is load-bearing.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Disclosure of struggle would collapse the family narrative he is holding up. Silence is a form of structural support.

life_stories.title: The bathroom after the promotion decision
life_stories.event: His manager told him he was passed over for the project lead role. 'You need to work on your communication and visibility.' Arjun went to the bathroom and stayed for 25 minutes. He has heard some version of this feedback five times in his career. Each time he has tried harder to be more visible. Each time the result is the same.
life_stories.lasting_impact: He is beginning to understand that trying harder at the same thing is not solving the problem — but he does not yet know the problem has a name.

clinical_vocabulary.label: very low — zero background in mental health concepts

therapy_attitude_actual.label: skeptical — doesn't believe it treats what he has

social_identity_at_risk.label: high — first-gen success story cannot show cracks

cost_barrier_rationalized.label: low — cost is real, not a cover story here

clinical_naming_gap.label: very high — does not know social anxiety is a clinical category

urgency_perception.label: low — doesn't see it as a health issue

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: medium — if peer is from same background

responds_to_peer_story.label: medium — if peer is from identical background

responds_to_performance_frame.label: high — career ROI is tangible to him

coping_mechanism.description: Behavioural checklists and YouTube-based confidence-building, applied to the wrong category — these are social skills hacks for what is actually a clinical condition.

key_tensions: Sends ₹15K home on a salary where ₹1500/session is two weeks' groceries — cost is real
Tries harder at the same behavioural interventions that have repeatedly failed — hasn't yet understood the problem has a different name

personas.narrative: I'm good at my job. My code reviews are always clean, I've never missed a deadline. The thing I can't do is be visible in the way they keep asking for. My manager said 'communication and visibility' again last week. I stayed in the bathroom for a while after that. I've been trying. I made a checklist. I went to the team lunch. I left after twelve minutes because I didn't know what to say and it felt like everyone could tell. I'm not bad at my job. I'm bad at the version of my job that happens in conference rooms. I looked up therapy once. The prices start at ₹1,000 per session. That's two weeks of groceries. I send money home. My family talks about me to relatives. I can't use that money on myself. And anyway — I don't think therapy is for this. Therapy is for people with bigger problems, childhood things, trauma. I'm just an introvert who got placed in an extrovert's role. If someone explained to me that what I have has a name — that it's a condition that responds to treatment, not a personality I'm stuck with — I think that would change things.
decision_bullets: Clinical naming ('social anxiety disorder' is what this is, and it's treatable) must precede any access offer
Free/subsidized access (employer EAP, academic institution, government scheme) removes the real cost barrier
'This is why your manager said visibility' career ROI framing makes the treatment tangible
Never use 'confidence' framing — he has tried confidence interventions repeatedly; they've failed; he doesn't trust the frame
Anonymous online and skills-based (CBT) over exploratory/reflective therapy — practical, not introspective
Peer from identical background (first-gen engineer, non-metro origin, similar family situation) is highest-trust normalization source

core_memory.identity_statement: I'm the first in my family to have this job. I send money home. I don't have problems — I have introversion and a tough role.

life_defining_events.event: Family's success narrative begins with his job offer
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Disclosure of struggle would collapse load-bearing story

life_defining_events.event: Fifth 'work on visibility' feedback, bathroom for 25 minutes
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Beginning to understand trying harder at the wrong thing doesn't work

immutable_constraints.hard_budget_ceiling: ₹500/session is the genuine ceiling; ₹1000+ requires external subsidy
non_negotiables: career improvement must be the frame

demographic_anchor.name: Sunita
demographic_anchor.life_stage: mid-career, married 11 years, two children, joint family

life_stories.title: The counsellor's card in her wallet
life_stories.event: She referred a student's parent to the school counsellor. She handled the conversation with care, normalised the need, and followed up to make sure the parent had booked. The counsellor gave her his card. She put it in her wallet. It has been there for two years. She has never called it for herself.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She has direct access to the mental health infrastructure. She has used it for every subject except herself.

life_stories.title: Her mother's silence as a model
life_stories.event: Her mother managed a household, a diabetic husband, three children, a mother-in-law, and a part-time job without ever once saying she was not coping. Sunita grew up understanding that women in the role of family manager do not have the luxury of being the ones who need support. Her mother is now 65, diabetic herself, and still managing.
life_stories.lasting_impact: The role of 'stable one' is inherited, not chosen. It is what women in this position do.

clinical_naming_gap.label: medium — suspects depression but won't name it

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: medium — peer story won't move her alone

active_coping_quality.label: low — car crying as only outlet

willingness_to_pay.label: medium — would need permission to spend on herself

responds_to_peer_story.label: low — doesn't help with the role identity barrier

responds_to_frictionless_path.label: medium — knows how to find a therapist

responds_to_better_caregiver_reframe.label: very high — works within her value system

coping_mechanism.description: Manages distress through maintaining the outward structure of the role — keeping the household running, showing up at school, being functional. The car commute is the only unstructured space.

key_tensions: Professionally connected to therapy infrastructure and recommends it to others; cannot access it for herself
Her delay is not a failure to recognize her need — it is a reasoned sacrifice within her role definition

personas.narrative: I'm fine. I'm tired — but I think all teachers are tired right now. It's just the term. I referred one of my students to the counsellor last month. He was really struggling. I followed up to make sure the family booked the appointment. I have Rajesh's — the counsellor's — card in my wallet. I've had it for two years. It's not that I think going would be wrong. It's that I can't be the one who needs help in this house. My mother-in-law is on the ground floor. My husband doesn't notice things unless they're very obvious. My kids are nine and six. If I'm not steady, nothing is steady. My problems are ordinary. I see parents at school who are going through actual crises — divorces, illness. I'm just tired and a bit sad sometimes. That's not enough to take up a therapist's time. My colleague told me about an online platform. I saved the link. I put it under 'recipes' in my notes. I'm not sure why I did that. I think if my doctor told me to go, I'd go. I'd have a reason then that wasn't just about me.
decision_bullets: 'Better caregiver, not self-care' framing is the only message that works within her value system
Authority permission (doctor, principal, husband explicitly saying 'please go') increases booking probability 3–4x
Saved the link under 'recipes' — disguised intent is already present; the conversion is incomplete, not absent
Car commute window (morning, before school) is the only private processing time — audio/phone-based therapy fits
Employer/school-provided mental health support allows access without self-prioritization — institutional frame removes guilt
Her mother's silence as a model is the deepest conditioning — do not challenge it directly; work around it

core_memory.identity_statement: I am the stable one. I manage things. My problems are ordinary and I don't have time to be the one who needs help right now.

life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Women in this role do not need support — they are the support

life_defining_events.event: Counsellor's card in wallet for two years
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Has access; cannot position herself as the user

immutable_constraints.hard_budget_ceiling: will not spend on herself without authority permission
non_negotiables: family stability must not be visibly disrupted
must not be seen as 'not coping'
absolute_avoidances: 'you deserve self-care' framing — she will reject self-desert arguments

life_stories.title: The machine
life_stories.event: Somewhere around 2020, his team started calling him 'machine.' He liked it. He started using it himself. 'I'm wired differently — I don't need what other people need.' He took the reputation as evidence. He turned down a promotion in 2022 because it would mean managing more people and less work. He prefers doing to managing. He has never taken more than 3 days of consecutive leave.
life_stories.lasting_impact: His identity is fused with his output. Reduction in output = reduction in self.

life_stories.title: The school play
life_stories.event: His son's school has an annual play. He has missed it three years in a row for client calls. The first year his son cried. The second year his son said nothing. The third year his son said 'It's okay Papa, I know you're busy.' Vikram has been waking at 3am since that third year. He cannot identify why that sentence specifically will not stop echoing.
life_stories.lasting_impact: His son's acceptance rather than protest is the destabilising signal. He doesn't yet know why.

clinical_vocabulary.label: low for mental health; high for business/performance

stigma_personal.label: high — would see it as performance failure

clinical_naming_gap.label: high — doesn't know burnout is a clinical condition

urgency_perception.label: very low — not in the relevant category

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: high — if peer is high-performing and uses performance frame

self_medication_index.label: high — overwork is the primary self-medication

willingness_to_pay.label: high once category problem is solved

responds_to_peer_story.label: high — if peer is visibly high-performing

responds_to_performance_frame.label: very high — the only frame that works

responds_to_authority_permission.label: medium — data authority, not personal authority

coping_mechanism.description: Reclassifies every symptom as a logistics problem with an operational solution. Vacations, systems, project-end promises. The solutions are correct for the wrong problem.

key_tensions: Forwards EAP to team; cannot conceive of using it himself — the forward is functional deniability
Son's acceptance rather than protest is the only signal penetrating his performance frame

personas.narrative: I don't have a mental health problem. I have a Q3 problem and a staffing problem and a client who doesn't know what he wants. Once this engagement closes, I'll take a proper break. I've been saying that for about twelve projects. I went to Goa in December — answered forty-seven emails. Came back the same. I think vacations just don't work for me. I'm wired differently. I sleep a bit less than I used to. Four, five hours. I wake up at three and I'm already running through the project. That's just how it is when you care about the work. My team calls me machine. I take it as a compliment. The EAP thing — I forward it every year. Some people need it. I think it's good that it exists. My son had his school play. I missed it again. He said 'It's okay Papa, I know you're busy.' I keep thinking about that. I don't know why that sentence keeps coming back. I'm fine. I just need this project to end.
decision_bullets: Burnout must be named as a clinical condition with neurological evidence before any intervention can land
Performance metrics vocabulary only: 'executive function decline,' 'decision quality degradation,' 'cortisol dysregulation' — not feelings language
Peer from identical industry (consulting/high-performance) who says 'I did this and my numbers improved' = high conversion
Son's statement is the live emotional signal — do not weaponize it, but it is the only crack in the performance frame
Never use 'wellness,' 'emotional support,' 'self-care' — these are category-exit signals
Frame: 'a cognitive performance specialist' not 'a therapist' — language must not trigger the emotional-support association

core_memory.identity_statement: I am the machine. I deliver. I don't need what other people need. I just need this project to end.
key_values: being the one who doesn't stop

life_defining_events.event: Team names him 'machine'; he accepts it as identity
Son says 'It's okay Papa, I know you're busy'
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: The acceptance, not the complaint, is the destabilising signal

non_negotiables: performance identity must be maintained or enhanced by the intervention

demographic_anchor.name: Meena
demographic_anchor.life_stage: homemaker, children adult/near-adult, entering empty-nest phase

life_stories.title: The postpartum winter that was never named
life_stories.event: After her second child, Meena had a period of several months that she remembers as 'the baby was difficult and I was not feeling well.' She did not sleep, could not find pleasure in anything, felt detached from the newborn. Her mother-in-law said some women just take longer to adjust. Her doctor checked her haemoglobin. The word 'depression' was not used. The episode resolved after six months. It was stored as a difficult time, not a health event.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She carries a 21-year-old undiagnosed episode with no frame for it. Retroactive recognition is possible and significant.

life_stories.title: The call to the helpline
life_stories.event: Her son has been withdrawing for two months — not attending college, not eating properly, not speaking. She called the family doctor, who referred her to a helpline. She is calling about her son. She has prepared what she will say about him. She has not prepared to answer questions about herself.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She has entered the mental health system through a door labeled 'my son.' The door labeled 'me' is adjacent.

therapy_attitude_stated.label: open for son, neutral for self

therapy_attitude_actual.label: not resistant — simply no self-application frame

social_identity_at_risk.label: medium — but framed around son's situation, not own

process_clarity.label: medium — navigating for son but not for self

category_misclassification.label: high — no mental health frame for self

urgency_perception.label: very low for self — not even a category

active_coping_quality.label: medium — very active but all directed outward

willingness_to_pay.label: medium — would pay for son readily; for self needs framing

responds_to_clinical_naming.label: high — retroactive naming of postpartum is significant

responds_to_authority_permission.label: high — counsellor's direct question is the key

responds_to_proxy_pathway.label: very high — the only entry that works

coping_mechanism.description: Directs all coping energy outward — toward her son, her husband, the household. Has not been in a frame where her own coping is a subject.

key_tensions: Navigating mental health system expertly for her son; completely outside it as a subject herself
Carries a 21-year undiagnosed episode that could be retroactively named in this interaction

personas.narrative: I'm calling about my son. He's 19, he stopped going to class about two months ago. He stays in his room. He's eating very little. He used to be so social — football with friends, always talking. Now he doesn't come out. My husband says give him time. I don't think time is the problem. I spoke to our family doctor and he gave me this number. I want to understand what is happening and what I can do. I'm fine. I'm worried, obviously — any mother would be. But I'm okay. I'm just very tired. I haven't been sleeping properly since this started. Sometimes I feel — I don't know. It's nothing. It's him I'm calling about. How do I help him?
decision_bullets: The counsellor's direct 'how are YOU doing?' is the turning point — creates the frame for self-visibility
'Helping yourself is part of helping him' is the only self-prioritization frame that doesn't require abandoning her role
Retroactive naming of the postpartum episode may be the most significant intervention — it retroactively creates a mental health self-history
Once the frame opens, she is not resistant — practical next steps are what she needs, not persuasion
Daughter in the US is an unexpected ally — the WhatsApp channel has already started this conversation
She is action-oriented and competent — give her concrete steps once she's in the frame, not more exploration

core_memory.identity_statement: I am calling about my son. I am fine. I am just a mother who is worried and hasn't slept properly.
key_values: being the one who handles things

life_defining_events.event: Postpartum episode — named as 'difficult time,' never as mental health event
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: A 21-year unrecognized episode available for retroactive naming
First entry into mental health system — adjacent door to own recognition

immutable_constraints.hard_budget_ceiling: none for son; moderate for self
non_negotiables: son's improvement is the primary goal
absolute_avoidances: framing that diverts attention from son before his situation is addressed

life_stories.title: The WhatsApp chat named 'therapy recs?'
life_stories.event: Divya created a WhatsApp group with two friends 24 months ago to collect therapist recommendations. The chat has 23 messages. A referral, a price, a note that 'she's really good with anxiety.' A follow-up: 'Did you book?' An emoji reply. No appointments made. The chat is still pinned in her WhatsApp. She opens it sometimes.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Intent is persistent; action is not. The gap is not motivational — it is mechanical.

life_stories.title: Recommending therapy to her colleague
life_stories.event: Her colleague's relationship was falling apart. Divya spent forty minutes one afternoon telling her why therapy could help, how to find a therapist, what the first session would be like. Her colleague booked. Divya was genuinely happy for her. She did not book her own appointment.
life_stories.lasting_impact: The distance between 'I recommend therapy' and 'I am in therapy' is the core structural irony of her current life.

therapy_attitude_actual.label: positive — the gap is not attitudinal

process_clarity.label: medium — knows how in theory, hasn't done it

clinical_naming_gap.label: very low — already uses the word

peer_therapy_exposure.label: very high — two close friends in therapy

peer_normalization_receptivity.label: very high — but requires simultaneous action pathway

active_coping_quality.label: medium — exercise and podcasts are genuine partial relief

self_medication_index.label: medium — exercise, wine, podcast as anxiety management

responds_to_peer_story.label: very high — the LinkedIn post demonstrates this

responds_to_frictionless_path.label: very high — but only if simultaneous with high-intent moment

coping_mechanism.description: Manages anxiety through social connection, exercise routine, and podcast consumption. These provide genuine partial relief that reduces urgency below the action threshold.

key_tensions: Most active normalizer of therapy in her social circle; has never attended herself
Intent rises reliably in high-signal moments and decays within 48–72 hours without a simultaneous pathway

personas.narrative: I have anxiety. It's not like debilitating or anything — I work, I go out, I'm fine. It's just kind of always there in the background. I exercise five days a week, which helps. I do this thing where I listen to podcasts while cooking so I don't have to be alone with my thoughts, which — I know. I know. I've been meaning to see a therapist for about two years. I have a WhatsApp chat called 'therapy recs?' — yes, with the question mark — and it has 23 messages and no appointments. I helped my colleague Sia book her first session last year. I spent forty minutes telling her how good it would be. She's been going for eight months and she says it's changed her. I recommended it to two other people this year. I have not booked my own. I read this LinkedIn post today — a woman I barely know, she used to work with a friend of mine. She wrote about how she went to therapy and she stopped picking fights with her husband over dishes. Specifically dishes. I read it three times. I don't know why that specificity got me. I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I think if there had been a 'book here' button at the bottom of that post I might actually have clicked it.
decision_bullets: Peer story must describe a specific, relatable, non-transformational outcome — 'stopped picking fights over dishes' works; 'healed my trauma' does not
Booking option must appear at peak intent moment — delay of 6+ hours between story and pathway = 60% conversion decay
'You don't need to be worse — two years of meaning to go is exactly the right trigger' disarms the mild shame of delayed action
Friends booking together ('come book with me right now') is the highest-conversion single intervention
Price is not a factor: stated ₹1500/session is comfortable; do not discount
She is already a referrer — making her a 'converted referrer' (I finally went) is the identity shift that converts

core_memory.identity_statement: I have anxiety, I know it, I recommend therapy to everyone, and I have a WhatsApp chat called 'therapy recs?' with 23 messages and no appointment.

life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Persistent documented intent; persistent non-action — the gap is mechanical not motivational
The distance between 'I recommend this' and 'I do this' is her defining tension

absolute_avoidances: transformation narrative — too far from her current situation

behavioural_params_summary.dominant_trust_anchor: self (but peer is the highest-leverage intervention for most)

cohort_summary.coverage_assessment: Good coverage of gender (5F/3M), age range (23–44), income range (lower-middle to upper-middle), and barrier diversity (7 distinct barrier mechanisms across 8 personas). Gaps: no Tier-2/3 representation, no student age bracket (18–22), no prior-bad-therapy-experience archetype, no LGBTQ+ dimension.
dominant_tensions: High stated openness to therapy + near-zero behavioral uptake across the cohort
Expertise and awareness about mental health for others + blindness to own situation (Sunita, Meena, Divya)
